text: The surgeon begins the robotic Roux-en-Y gastric bypass by placing trocars at the angle of His, creating space using a perigastric technique approximately five centimeters below the diaphragm. Careful dissection is performed to avoid bleeding. The first stapler fire is made transversely, two to three centimeters wide, with a vessel sealer used to dissect into the lesser sac. A bougie is placed to guide the stapler firing, creating a gastric pouch. Two stay sutures are placed: the posterior row outer layer with 2-0 Stratafix and a Vicryl suture to hold the pouch down.

The surgeon divides the thick omentum and reflects the colon upwards to locate the ligament of Treitz. A running posterior row suture line is created through the staple line using previously placed sutures. The surgeon uses scissors on a cut setting to perform a gastrotomy and enterotomy, ensuring clean entry. Two ovicryl sutures are tied together to create the inner layer anastomosis with full thickness bites, starting from either corner or the center. The suture is canal-backed to meet at the correct spot for anterior closure, primarily sewing forehand.

The surgeon snugly ties the sutures together to secure the inner layer of the anastomosis, using Vicryl sutures. A two O Stratafix suture is then introduced to close the anterior outer layer of the anastomosis.

The surgeon checks the patency of the anastomosis by passing a bougie through it. Then, the BP limb is separated from the Roux limb by creating a window in the mesentery. Seam guard is used as a handle during this process.

The surgeon measures approximately 100 centimeters to create a side-to-side jejuno-jejunostomy. Using the cut setting on six, enterotomies are made without placing stay sutures. The defect is closed by hand sewing with a running one-layer suture using 2-0 Vicryl, starting from the opposite corner to ensure complete closure.
The surgeon ties the closed anastomosis to the first stay suture and proceeds to close the mesenteric window using a two O Ethabon suture.
The surgeon cinches down the sutures to ensure the space is closed, performing a leak test by introducing oxygen through an orogastric tube, while occluding the limb.
